data.title: Tech Rebalance And Volatility Catalysts: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Index Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Stocks are back to quite a decent outlook, and markets are all out on yet another phase of the debasement trade, which just took silver to $100 and gold to $5,000.

However, as we head into the weekend, some factors of uncertainty could create significant volatility and change the current market picture in high fashion. One of the first and most evident yet easily overlooked is the federal chair appointment announced by the US President.

As evidenced in this remarkable piece, Trump suggested a decision around his return to the White House from Davos (and he just came back). The race is between Rick Rieder, BlackRock's CEO, and a well-positioned Wall Street veteran who could be the surprise. His appointment would reassure the US dollar and bond markets the most.

On the other hand, Kevin Warsh, the heavy favorite, an ex-Governor at the Fed from 2006-2011 (rough period), could maintain the current trend. Any communications regarding the decision will be market-rocking.

A second risk catalyst for this weekend is the high potential for geopolitical catalysts as we conclude a period full of headlines – heavy US military assets are concentrating in the Middle East, and my thesis is that the Greenland issue was being offered as a distraction.

On that aspect, oil is up 2.50% and still rebounding at its risk premium support area. We will see more on this this weekend – if nothing happens, expect stocks to keep up their path higher.

A third potential volatility contributor could be current market levels – with gold reaching $5,000, silver exploding by 250% to $100, and stock markets at all-time highs, one small profit-taking wave can turn into a significant selling cascade, particularly as we approach the FOMC.

All three factors, when combined, could significantly affect the market picture, so keep an eye on how they interact.

In terms of today's action, US consumer sentiment just beat expectations, and inflation expectations cooled down, usually a positive for the market, but with key tech earnings (helping Nasdaq to outperform) and the Fed meeting approaching (January 28), don't expect a straightforward path ahead.

As a reminder, Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), Tesla (TSLA), and ASML (ASML) report after the close on FOMC day, while Apple (AAPL) and Visa (V) follow on Thursday.

Magnificent 7s and semiconductors are doing the heavy lifting in today's sessions. Microsoft is leading its peers to the upside, while defensives and financials drag lower as their earnings season is now complete for the most part.

Dive into our daily session charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500 – watch for weekend volatility as the situation is not fully clear yet and Iran remains a factor.

Dow Jones 4H Chart

The Dow just rejected its all-time highs, not so surprising as we head into the weekend, and the direction has mostly stayed rangebound since 2026. The range, extending between 48,000 and 49,700 provides levels of interest for upcoming action:

  • A clear break above should see fast-paced continuation, first to the 50,000 milestone and surely higher (particularly if the recent doubts and uncertainties get resolved).
  • Any weekly close below 48,000 can precede more downside, potentially extending to 45,000.
    • For the short run, watch if the current retracement stalls at the 4H 200-MA (48,663), which would be the most bullish corrective outlook

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Morning highs: 49,663
  • Short Timeframe resistance 49,200 to 49,300
  • 49,650 to 49,670 Current ATH Resistance
  • All-time highs: 49,710
  • 50,000 Potential Psychological Resistance

Support Levels

  • 49,041 4H 20-period MA
  • Christmas ATH High Timeframe Momentum Pivot – 49,000
  • Intraday support 48,600 to 48,700 (4H 200-period MA)
  • Psychological support and range lows at 48,000
  • 45,000 psychological level (main support on higher timeframe)

Nasdaq 4H Chart

Nasdaq is now taking the lead (for the first time in a while) in today's action, as traders reposition ahead of next week's high-expectation earnings.

The pressure is high for the tech-heavy index: These early 2026 reports will reflect on this current year's projections and 2025 numbers as P/E Ratios reach elevated levels, particularly for Mag 7s.

The index is still showing a decent rebound in today's session, and looking at the previous days' action, a breakout of its 24,000 to 25,800 range could be close. A measured move hints at a retest of the all time high records.

This will, of course, need to be accompanied by positive (or at least not too pessimistic) fundamentals – a break and close above the 25,850 level should see continuation.

Next week's earnings will have a key influence on the Nasdaq movement. Keep a close eye on the Fed's communications next week.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Session highs: 25,733 (range highs, key test)
  • Intermediate Resistance 25,700 to 25,850
  • All-time high resistance zone 26,100 to 26,300
  • Current ATH: 26,182

Support Levels

  • Momentum Pivot 25,200 to 25,500 +/- 75 pts
  • Session lows: 25,428
  • Minor Support 25,000 to 25,250
  • 24,500 Main support
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 Support

S&P 500 4H Chart

The S&P is still contained within its main 6,800 to 6,975 range - showing similar conditions as the Nasdaq; expect similar breakout scenarios:

  • A break above the 7,000 all time highs with volume should lead to continued upside.
  • A rejection and close below the 6,800 key support should see profit-taking acceleration.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Session highs: 6,938
  • Previous ATH Resistance 6,945 to 6,975
  • Current ATH Resistance at 7,000

Support Levels

  • Pivot Zone 6,880 to 6,900

Safe trades and a blessed weekend!

data.title: Run It Like It's Hot
data.description:
By Christopher Puplava, CRPC

Stay the Course or Take Profits?

Last year capped a remarkable three-year run for equities, with the S&P 500 rising roughly 86% since the close of the 2022 mini-bear market. After such strong gains, a natural question arises: is it time to take profits and protect accumulated returns, or remain invested for further upside?

To answer this, we must ground our outlook in economic fundamentals and objective market signals. Based on supportive monetary and fiscal policy, both here and abroad, we believe the stage is set for continued economic growth in 2026. This is also a U.S. mid-term election year, and with political control at stake, policy incentives are aligned toward sustaining economic momentum and financial market stability. In short, conditions favor running the economy “hot,” not cooling it.

While our outlook for 2026 remains bullish, as capital allocators, we must remain vigilant to bear market risks. Protecting client capital requires understanding what types of bear markets exist, how they form, and whether current conditions resemble their historical precursors.

Understanding Bear Markets

Historically, bear markets fall into three broad categories:

  1. Cyclical bear markets, driven by economic slowdowns or recessions that reduce consumer spending and corporate earnings.
  2. Structural bear markets, which are longer and deeper, such as the Great Depression of the 1930s, the inflationary bear market of the 1970s, or the tech bubble collapse in the early 2000s.
  3. Event-driven bear markets, which are rarer and typically short-lived, such as the 1998 Asian Currency Crisis, the LTCM collapse, or the 2020 COVID-19 shock.

Event-driven declines are difficult to predict and typically recover quickly. Cyclical and structural bear markets, however, usually exhibit identifiable warning signs — notably monetary tightening, liquidity withdrawal, deteriorating credit conditions, or extreme valuations combined with policy restraint. Aside from high valuations, the other conditions are not present today.

Valuations Are High — But Liquidity Remains Supportive

By traditional valuation measures, the U.S. stock market is expensive. Using the S&P 500 price-to-sales ratio as a simple yardstick:

  • Tech bubble peak (2000): ~2x sales
  • Pre-financial crisis peak (2007): ~1.5x sales
  • Pre-2022 mini-bear market peak: ~3x sales
  • Current level: ~3.4x sales — the highest in more than 35 years

One could reasonably argue that U.S. equities display bubble-like valuation characteristics. However, valuation alone does not end bull markets. Historically, major peaks occur when elevated valuations collide with monetary tightening — that is, when the Federal Reserve “takes away the punch bowl.”

That is not today’s environment. The Fed is cutting interest rates and expanding its balance sheet by approximately $40 billion per month — a stark contrast to the tightening that preceded both the 2000 and 2007 peaks and contributed to the 2022 mini bear market. In our view, this makes the probability of a near-term structural bear market low.

Most cyclical bear markets are also preceded by monetary tightening. Instead, current conditions show multiple forms of ongoing stimulus:

  • Central bank easing via lower short-term rates
  • Commercial bank lending at a three-year high
  • Mortgage rates declining from ~8% in 2023 to just above 6% today
  • Falling oil and gasoline prices
  • Fiscal stimulus via an estimated $200B in consumer tax rebates

These forces point toward economic acceleration, not contraction.

Market Internals: The Leaves on the Tree

The stock market provides continuous feedback for those willing to listen. One useful analogy is a tree through changing seasons. When autumn approaches, leaves change color and begin to fall — long before the tree appears bare. Similarly, bear markets often begin internally: individual stocks weaken while headline indices still rise. Eventually, more stocks fall than rise, and the broader market follows.

Currently, we see the opposite. Participation across the market is stronger than at any point last year. A large percentage of stocks in the Russell 3000 remain above their 200-day moving averages — a key measure of long-term trend health. Credit markets, which typically deteriorate before stock markets fall, show no stress; credit spreads remain contained.

Broad participation and healthy credit conditions are hallmarks of economic acceleration. These signals validate, rather than contradict, our bullish outlook.

Positioning: “Skate to where the puck is going to be, not where it has been” – Wayne Gretzky

Our bullish thesis is currently demonstrated by our stock exposure near maximum thresholds, with an underweight toward bonds. The risk of an overheating economy is renewed inflation, which we hedge against through our commodity exposure, as well as a reduced exposure toward bonds, as rising inflation is typically coupled with rising bond yields, which hurt bond prices.

Beyond asset class exposure, we have deliberately taken profits in positions that have significantly appreciated and are no longer undervalued, allowing us to seek out new opportunities with greater value potential. As mentioned previously, just as there are tells for the changing seasons, we see tells for the changing business cycle. One area that is often the first to detect a shift in monetary policy is precious metals, which had a banner year last year and correctly foresaw the Fed shifting toward an easing policy but also likely benefited from geopolitical uncertainty.

Monetary and fiscal stimulus influence the economy and markets with a lag. In 2019, gold took off before the Fed began to expand its balance sheet and was quick to respond to the Fed’s intervention during the COVID pandemic of 2020, rallying to an all-time high of over $2,000 an ounce. However, just as the economy was starting to come back in mid-2020, gold peaked and sold off 20% into 2021. There was a clear handoff from precious metals to other commodities like energy and base metals as the economy came roaring back, where the CRB Raw Industrials Index (composed of base metals, textiles, and raw materials) rallied over 70% in the following two years (lower panel below).

Just as gold and precious metals outperformed other commodities during the 2019–2020 cycle, they have once again taken the lead. After a period of stagnation since 2020, gold broke out to an all-time high in 2024 (top panel below) and has delivered a remarkable performance since then. In our view, gold appears to be leading other commodities by roughly two years, with the broader CRB Raw Industrials Index recently breaking out of a three-year base (bottom panel) and now potentially poised to retest its 2022 highs.

Precious metals—particularly gold—have historically acted as early indicators of broader capital shifts. When we push gold forward by two years, we can see how the CRB Raw Industrials Index appears to be following gold’s path, which is one of the reasons our firm has diversified our commodity exposure.

Most notably, this two-year lead between gold and the broader commodity complex is also seen with stocks. Looking at the GDX Gold Miners ETF (red line below, advanced two years), we can see a strong two-year leading correlation with energy stocks using the XOP Oil & Gas ETF (black line below). We believe the puck will be skating toward other commodities beyond precious metals, which is why we have shifted our commodity exposure. Depending on the client's objective, we have exposure to copper, aluminum, iron ore, agriculture and energy.

Beyond commodities, in the last few months, we have also reallocated away from the technology sector and toward more value-oriented sectors that follow the business cycle, such as industrials and consumer stocks. Growth stocks like technology companies tend to outperform value stocks when overall economic growth is anemic or declining and command a premium for their persistent growth. However, value stocks tend to have volatile earnings that ebb and flow with the economy, and so when growth is not scarce and the economy accelerates, they often outperform growth stocks. This is why we have shifted to value stocks, considering our bullish economic outlook.

While a strengthening economy is a key catalyst for potential outperformance of value stocks over growth stocks this year, we may be approaching a significant regime shift—one in which value stocks could outperform growth not only in the near term but potentially for years to come. If our assessment of this regime shift proves accurate, it will be imperative for portfolio managers—many of whom have maintained significant overweights in technology and growth sectors over the past decade—to recognize and adapt to this evolving market environment.

Is the US Dollar in Another Secular Bear Market?

While value stocks typically outperform growth stocks in an accelerating economy, they also tend to excel during periods of U.S. Dollar (USD) weakness. This is primarily because commodity-related sectors—such as energy and materials—are classified as value stocks and are more heavily represented in value indexes than in growth indexes. These sectors stand to benefit from a weaker USD.

Additionally, value sectors generally have a higher proportion of export-oriented businesses compared to growth sectors. As the USD declines, revenues generated abroad—when converted back into dollars—increase, providing a tailwind for these sectors. This dynamic is a key reason why value stocks tend to benefit in a weak USD environment.

This relationship is evident when comparing the USD Index (DXY, black line below) with the relative performance of the S&P 500 Growth Index (SGX) versus the S&P 500 Value Index (SVX) (red line below). In the 1990s, as the USD appreciated, growth stocks outperformed value. Conversely, during the 2000s, a declining USD boosted commodity prices and disproportionately benefited value stocks. However, following the stabilization of the USD Index in 2008—marking the end of a prolonged secular bear market—growth stocks regained leadership and outperformed value for nearly two decades as the USD entered a secular bull market.

Just as getting the economy correct is important for asset allocation and portfolio construction, so too is getting the direction of the USD. We have seen three major secular bear markets and three major secular bull markets in the USD Index over the last half century, as shown below. We are closely monitoring the USD Index in the months ahead to quickly identify any major secular shift—either confirming that our current portfolio allocations remain appropriate or signaling the need to adjust, such as reducing commodity exposure and reallocating toward growth sectors.

When we zoom in on the USD and its recent secular bull market, we can see that it began in 2011 and has been in a well-defined rising trend channel. When it hits the bottom of the channel, it rises, and when it hits the top end of the channel, it falls. The USD Index had a major breakout in 2022 by surging to multi-decade highs when it exceeded the $100 price level (horizontal red line below). That key level remained as support for the next three years, as it rallied every time it tested $100. However, in mid-2025, that level was broken and has remained key resistance to every USD rally since then. The USD Index is currently being squeezed between this key $100 resistance level and the rising secular lower trend channel. A break below this level would confirm our suspicion that the secular bull market in the USD that began in 2011 is over, where 2022 marked the top, and that we are now in a secular bear market for the USD, likely for years to come.

The Demographic Dollar Decline

While this is our thesis, we must have corroboration and will adapt client portfolios should our belief prove premature. The catalyst that could end the secular bull market for the USD and help explain gold’s performance is a major expansion in the Federal Reserve’s balance sheet and the supply of USDs. The Federal Reserve, in its own research papers, predicted that it would have to expand its balance sheet and keep its size roughly 20% of the economy as measured by Gross Domestic Product (GDP). However, if foreign demand for U.S. Treasuries (USTs) and the USD continues to decline, the Fed may need to increase asset purchases even further, effectively expanding the money supply at an accelerated pace.

Foreign official holdings of U.S. Treasuries have declined from over $3 trillion in 2021 to $2.7 trillion today. While this reduction may appear modest, it is significant in the context of the U.S. public debt expanding from $28 trillion to $38 trillion over the same period. In other words, as the U.S. increased its outstanding debt by $10 trillion in the past five years, foreign official institutions not only refrained from absorbing any of this new issuance but were actually net sellers of Treasuries.

Given current demographic trends, entitlement spending for Medicare and Social Security is projected to accelerate, driving even larger federal deficits in the years ahead. If foreign demand for U.S. debt continues to decline, financing these deficits will become increasingly challenging. Since 2020, China has reduced its U.S. Treasury holdings by over $400 billion, leaving Japan as the largest foreign creditor with approximately $1.2 trillion in holdings. However, Japan’s holdings have remained flat over the past five years, and with its own rising debt burden and increasing domestic bond yields, there is a real risk that Japanese investors may repatriate capital to support their local bond market. Together, these twin demographic and fiscal pressures in both the U.S. and Japan could be the primary catalysts propelling the U.S. dollar into a prolonged secular decline through the end of the decade.

Risks and Opportunities

Given the level of U.S. indebtedness and the ongoing increase in debt issuance, a key risk we continue to monitor is the potential for a significant breakout in bond yields. Over the past three years, the 30-year U.S. Treasury yield has remained range-bound between approximately 4% and 5%. A breakout to new highs in the 30-year yield would likely exert downward pressure on the equity market, especially if accompanied by a strengthening U.S. dollar. Notably, the combination of rising interest rates (red line below) and a surging USD (green line below) contributed to the 2022 equity bear market (black line, red-shaded region). Conversely, as interest rates and the USD Index peaked in October 2022, equity markets stabilized and the bear market came to an end (yellow shaded region).

These two areas, bond yields and the USD, will be our key tells for whether to maintain an aggressive stance or shift course and raise cash as we navigate 2026.

As is widely recognized, Fed Chairman Jerome Powell’s term concludes in May, providing President Trump with the opportunity to appoint a successor. Given that this is a mid-term election year, it is highly probable that Trump will select a candidate who shares his preference for lower interest rates to stimulate economic growth. Chairman Powell initiated a rate-cutting cycle in 2024, which could readily continue under a more dovish Fed Chair. Even in the absence of additional cuts, the lagged effects of the 2024–2025 easing cycle may still influence financial markets this year, potentially serving as a bullish catalyst.

Historically, changes in the Federal Reserve’s policy rate have influenced investor behavior with a two-year lag, drawing capital out of financial markets and into money market funds during tightening cycles and encouraging movement out of cash and back into risk assets during easing cycles. This relationship is illustrated below with the Federal Funds Rate (black line, advanced two years) and money market assets (blue line). Currently, nearly $8 trillion is parked in money market funds, and 2026 falls within the historical window when investors and savers may begin reallocating from cash to financial markets. With President Trump signaling a desire to “run the economy hot,” this potential rotation of capital could provide a further boost to equities, especially in the context of a strong economic backdrop.

Summary

In summary, the investment landscape entering 2026 remains favorable for continued economic expansion and equity market strength. Supportive monetary and fiscal policy, improving credit conditions, broadening market participation, and accelerating economic momentum all reinforce our constructive outlook. While valuations are elevated and risks always exist, the historical precursors to major cyclical or structural bear markets are not presently in place.

Accordingly, we remain positioned to participate in further upside while maintaining disciplined risk management and closely monitoring key market “tells” that would signal the need to adjust course. Our focus remains steadfast: protecting capital during periods of stress while capturing potential opportunities during periods of expansion.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: China: Repositioning For The Next Phase Of Global Industrialization
data.description:
In 2025, China’s economic policy reached an inflection point, actively encouraging technology and manufacturing companies to move up the value chain while taking a more hands-off approach to the property market and domestic consumption.

As a result, China’s exports of these industries surged in 2025, helping Chinese equity markets outperform other major markets.

We asked two of our China investment experts - Vivian Lin Thurson, CFA, partner, and Clifford Chi-wai Lau, CFA - to discuss their outlook for 2026.

What are the main policy developments in China over the past 12 months that investors should be aware of?

Vivian: The biggest change is that the Chinese government has begun to decisively support innovation and technological advancement in areas such as AI, electric vehicles (EVs), and automation. The era of restrictions on China’s major internet and technology companies appears to be over.

This is a strategic move to upgrade China’s position in the economic value chain from labor-intensive to innovation- and technology-led industries. This will likely be an ongoing, longer-term initiative, and U.S. trade pressure on China has further catalyzed these efforts as China realizes it needs to become more technologically self-reliant. China also understands that as its economy gets bigger, growth will slow structurally, and the country needs new growth engines.

By contrast, government support for other parts of the economy has been rather muted. The country’s broad-based economy continues to struggle, whether you look at consumption, the property market, or domestic infrastructure and manufacturing investments. The government has taken relatively minor steps to support these areas but not nearly enough to make a major impact.

Despite a resumption of the U.S.-China trade war, Chinese exports on a global basis have continued to rise as China broadens its exports to other regions. U.S. export volume is still large in absolute terms, but the growth has been coming from exports to the rest of the world. Strong exports have helped alleviate pressure on the domestic side of the economy.

Clifford: Reading between the lines of the new five-year plan the Chinese government issued in October 2025, the government seems set on reallocating resources from low-value manufacturing to high-value technology and services. I expect more capital expenditure to be directed towards building out advanced chip technology, for example. This could be the leading driver of Chinese economic activity in the next few years.

With consumer confidence depressed, the government has undertaken some short-term initiatives to encourage spending—subsidizing upgrades to electrical appliances and smartphones, for example. These types of measures are not very sustainable, whereas a longer-term policy to transition to a higher-value-added position in the value chain could be.

A year ago, China was struggling with macroeconomic weakness on several fronts including the property sector, local authority finance, and consumer confidence. What are the latest developments in these areas?

Vivian: In China’s property market, we’ve seen some stabilization in tier 1[1] cities and certain outlying regions. However, overall demand for property remains weak. Youth unemployment also remains a problem, and consumer confidence is still low.

Given the weakness in the broader economy, you could ask why the government has not been more aggressive with stimulus. Again, I think they are encouraging a structural rebalancing of the economy, pushing it higher up the value chain and trying to find new pillars of growth such as innovation and technology. If the property market can’t support China’s economy as it did in the past, why stimulate it?

At the same time, the broad-based China economy continues to face deflationary pressures; producer prices have experienced their longest streak of monthly declines since China’s market reforms of the late 1970s and early 1980s.

China’s Economy: A Tale of Two Cities

China’s growing dominance in the global EV market is just one example of the country’s momentum in innovation- and technology-led manufacturing.

Sources: BofA Global Research, National Bureau of Statistics of China, Bloomberg, and William Blair, as of December 2024.

At the same time, the broad-based China economy continues to face deflationary pressures; producer prices have experienced their longest streak of monthly declines since China's market reforms of the late 1970s and early 1980s.

Sources: BofA Global Research, National Bureau of Statistics of China, Bloomberg, and William Blair, as of December 2024.

How do you expect the Chinese economy to perform in 2026?

Clifford: As China pursues its goal of rebalancing the economy to higher-value manufacturing, it’s doubtful that policy makers will look to push for aggressive growth.

The government’s unofficial gross domestic product (GDP) growth target is 5%, and we’ve reduced our own expectations and set a lower bound of 4.5%. It’s more realistic and gives the government more space to operate while executing this rebalancing act.

Vivian: It’s possible real GDP growth could hit 5% in 2025 as exports have accelerated, with some trade being pulled forward because of the trade war. Exports may decelerate a bit in 2026, but they should remain a strong growth driver.

We’re less sure about the strength of China’s domestic economy, but base effect should start getting better. Over the next 5 to 10 years, China’s annual GDP growth will likely decline to 3.5% to 4.0%, which is a natural development as absolute GDP gets bigger.

How has the U.S.–China trade war affected China’s export activity?

Vivian: The United States is still one of China’s largest trading partners, with gross trades reaching approximately $650 billion in 2024, but Chinese exports to the United States have been in decline since the first U.S.-China trade war in 2018. However, China’s gross exports to the rest of the world have increased over 40% during the same period.

EVs provide a prime example of how China is globalizing its high-value industrialization. Chinese manufacturers have come up with technologically advanced, user friendly, and aesthetically appealing EVs at affordable prices and are now leading global EV development. These EVs are an especially good solution for emerging countries. And we’re seeing similar trends in Europe, with Chinese EVs accounting for approximately 13% of the EV market in Western Europe.

Given the weakness of the domestic economy, Chinese companies have had to seek growth outside China. The government is encouraging them to go abroad to support exports and take up the slack in the domestic economy.

How have Chinese equity markets performed in 2025?

Vivian: Chinese equity markets have outperformed other major markets. The MSCI China Index[2] - mainly Hong Kong-listed ADRs and some China A shares—was up around 37% year-to-date as of mid-November 2025. This compares to about 15.7% for the S&P 500 Index[3], 19% for the MSCI World Index[4], and 32% for the MSCI Emerging Markets (EM) Index[5].

We’re seeing a long-overdue rerating of Chinese equities. You can point to several catalysts - inflows sparked by distressed valuations and a weak U.S. dollar, benign monetary and fiscal policy, government support for innovation, and the headlines from DeepSeek in January 2025, which is when global investors started to recognize China’s AI progress. We’ve also seen an improvement in tech and internet company fundamentals and earnings.

The U.S.–China trade war is another factor. Chinese equities sold off sharply after the so-called “Liberation Day” at the beginning of April but have outperformed since. This could be a reflection that investors are betting that President Trump’s harsh anti-China rhetoric is mainly a negotiating position. The October 30, 2025, meeting between President Trump and President Xi, the first in six years, seemed amicable, and it looks as if there was agreement in several key areas.

As a quality growth investor, where do you currently see the most compelling opportunities in the Chinese equity market?

Vivian: Our focus is on innovation and technology. I believe AI will likely continue to be the key global investment theme. In our view, we're still in the early innings in China, but recently, we’ve seen companies such as Alibaba (BABA), ByteDance (BDNCE), and Tencent (TCEHY) - which are becoming Chinese hyperscalers - step up AI spending. We expect their stock prices to benefit, first through sentiment and momentum and then through earnings traction.

E-commerce continues to be very competitive, so it’s important to pick winners and losers carefully. Alibaba, for example, is starting to turn around its e-commerce business, helped by improved regulatory backdrop, its AI capabilities, and a new CEO who is implementing some encouraging initiatives.

We’re also looking at AI-related tech hardware on two fronts: companies servicing the global AI supply chain, supplying Nvidia (NVDA) or global hyperscalers such as Google and Meta, and domestic AI development. China is likely to decouple from the rest of the world in AI systems, partly because of restrictions placed on companies such as Huawei, and thus has had to develop its own domestic supply chain.

The EV supply chain is another focus area for us. There are interesting developments in autonomous driving, and although EV growth remains a compelling top-down story, intense competition continues to pressure margins, especially for the EV original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).

Bottom-up, it’s harder to find strength, but we believe there could be some winners along the value chain, such as EV battery maker Contemporary Amperex Technology Co. Limited (CTATF). It is the largest global EV battery producer with world-leading technology and agnostic to any EV manufacturer, which puts it in a strong position.

We also like biotech, specialty pharma, and contract research development and manufacturing organizations (CRDMOs). Chinese companies in this space have been out of favor for the last few years, but as policy and geopolitical risk has declined and demand picked up in the global industry, they are starting to show tremendous earnings recovery. They have showcased to the world how much progress China has made on cutting-edge cancer drugs.

How have Chinese bond markets performed this year, and what do you expect in 2026?

Clifford: It has not been an especially exciting year for the Chinese government bond (CGB) market. The 10-year CGB is now yielding 1.5%, which is not particularly attractive, especially when there is no reason to expect a sharp increase in economic activity.

I don’t believe the government is concerned about inflation, so we do not expect interest rates to go much higher, and yields are likely to be range-bound. There may be some steepening in the yield curve, both from natural pressure to normalize and selling activity from local investors reducing fixed-income allocations to catch the rally in Chinese equities.

In this environment, we believe there may be opportunities to trade the range if the yield curve steepens or the market consolidates or corrects, but these shifts would likely be minor. In general, we believe investors should consider limiting CGB holdings and turn those allocations to higher-beta or higher-yielding Asian or EMs.

China Local Yields Stay Unattractive Compared to Other EM Benchmarks

Chinese government bond yields are among the lowest of all major EMs. Low yields are likely to remain as long as China’s domestic economy remains soft and deflationary pressures prevail.

Sources: J.P. Morgan and William Blair, as of October 2025. Local currency yields are represented by the J.P. Morgan Government Bond Index—Emerging Markets (GBI-EM) suite of indices, which track local-currency sovereign bonds issued by EM countries. (Index information has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable but J.P. Morgan does not warrant its completeness or accuracy. The indices are used with permission. The indices may not be copied, used, or distributed without J.P. Morgan’s prior written approval. Copyright 2024, JPMorgan Chase & Co. All rights reserved.)

What is your outlook for the renminbi over the next year or so?

Clifford: We expect the renminbi to remain fairly stable versus other currencies. There is no pressure on the government to devalue because exports have been doing fine without this intervention.

There may be modest technical upside as China continues its efforts to build a currency bloc with EM countries and promote the renminbi as an alternative reserve currency. The share of global trade settled in renminbi has been rising, but it is a very long way from the scale of a reserve currency such as the U.S. dollar, which is incredibly liquid, highly transparent, and without capital controls. It is unlikely China can match that, especially not now.

[1] In China, a tier 1 city refers to the country’s most economically developed, internationally connected, and influential urban centers such as Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, and Shenzhen.

[2] The MSCI China Index captures large- and mid-cap representation across China A shares, H shares, B shares, Red chips, P chips, and foreign listings (such as ADRs).

[3] The Standard & Poor’s (S&P) 500 Index tracks the performance of 500 large companies listed on stock exchanges in the United States.

[4] The MSCI World Index measures the performance of large- and mid-cap stocks across 23 developed markets, representing about 85% of their total market capitalization.

[5] The MSCI Emerging Markets (EM) Index captures large- and mid-cap representation across 27 EMs.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Equipment Rental Rises While Crypto Company Crashes In 2-IPO Week
data.description:
Two operating companies priced IPOs this week, along with six SPACs.

Digital asset infrastructure provider BitGo (BTGO) priced its US IPO above the range to raise $213 million at a $2.2 billion market cap. The company provides a digital asset custody, lending, and infrastructure platform serving institutional clients. Despite pricing up and initially opening with a nearly 25% gain, BitGo crumbled in the aftermarket, finishing the week 19% below offer.

Fast-growing construction equipment rental platform EquipmentShare.com (EQPT) raised $747 million at a $6.7 billion market cap, pricing at the midpoint. The company rents and sells construction equipment through its digital platform and over 300 stores. Shares surged 33% on day one.

data.title: Uber: A Platform The Market Still Underestimates
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The market undervalues how hard Uber’s scale is to compete with, while placing disproportionate attention on autonomy and regulatory concerns that are distant and uncertain. As a result, Uber’s improving earnings power and cash generation are not fully reflected in the current valuation.

Company Overview

Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER) is a global technology company that offers services across mobility, delivery, and freight. The business connects independent service providers with consumers and enterprises. Uber does not own any vehicles or inventory; instead, they provide the infrastructure to allow the real time matching of supply and demand.

Industry Overview

The ride-hailing industry has expanded rapidly, reaching annual revenue figures of ~$180 billion, and is projected to grow to ~$230 billion by the end of the decade. This results from the combination of a more densely concentrated urban population, the steadily increasing cost of car ownership, and the widespread adoption of digital platforms worldwide.

The competition in the ride-hailing industry is oligopolistic and highly regional, with a small number of providers dominating in their respective geographic markets. This competition structure arises from the substantial entry barriers posed to market entrants. These include the high fixed costs associated with both regulatory compliance and quality software, as well as the strong network effects required for efficient matching between riders and drivers.

The development of autonomous vehicles represents a long-term structural shift within the industry. If firms are able to integrate this into their model, they will remove their biggest expense, labour. However, AVs will likely create a more fragmented marketplace, giving consumers more choice and firms less pricing power.

Competitor Landscape

Uber is increasingly approaching Google-like dominance, where market share translates disproportionately into service quality and leaves the industry unviable for new entrants without either a misstep by Uber or innovation well beyond what competitors have historically demonstrated.

In 2022, Uber had captured roughly 25% of the global ride-hailing market share, with their closest competitor, Lyft, Inc. (LYFT), having captured 8%. In the United States specifically, Uber holds a staggering ¾ of the entire market. Uber is approaching a level of scale where its network effects are no longer just an advantage but a barrier in their own right. Uber’s scale improves liquidity, matching efficiency, and reliability. Higher share directly produces a better service, and that better service mechanically drives further share gains.

Autonomous Vehicles

Investors are becoming increasingly concerned about the impact of AVs on Uber's model, and rightly so. The introduction of these driverless vehicles could completely change the marketplace dynamics, offering competitors such as Tesla the ability to offer substantially cheaper ride prices than their human driven alternatives.

However, concerns that AVs will significantly disrupt Uber tend to assume an abrupt shift from human drivers to full automation, which is not a realistic outcome. A report by BCG forecasts that by 2035, in a base case scenario, 60 cities globally will have "large AV deployment". It cites Phoenix as one of the 6 cities globally to have reached this threshold as early as 2024. However, as of late 2025, this "large fleet" consists of only ~500 vehicles.

Crucially, these vehicles introduced will be Level 4 autonomy, which means they are only able to operate under a very strict domain. They rely on pre-mapped roads, limited geographies, and specific environmental conditions. Level 5 autonomy, where a vehicle can drive anywhere that a human can, in all conditions, currently does not exist.

When the required pre-mapping is combined with the need for city-by-city regulatory approval, it strongly suggests that AV deployment will remain highly concentrated in a small number of carefully chosen cities. In practice, this creates a fairly binary outcome: a city either has a meaningful, tightly defined AV presence, or it has none at all. Given the time and cost involved in mapping roads and securing regulatory approval, it makes far more sense for operators to expand coverage within cities where that work is already done, rather than starting from scratch in new markets.

In practice, based on the BCG report, only around 60 out of the 15,000+ cities Uber operates in will have significant AV deployment by 2035. This implies that the overwhelming majority of Uber's network will continue to rely on human drivers.

This slow adoption gives Uber significant time and flexibility to adapt and partner with third-party suppliers. Importantly, Uber is not passive in this transition. As AV availability increases, Uber can incrementally integrate autonomous vehicles into the platform while human drivers continue to serve areas, times, and use cases where automation remains uneconomic or impractical.

The introduction of AVs is likely to complement Uber rather than displace it altogether. Autonomous vehicles reduce the cost per trip and improve reliability, but they do not eliminate the need for a platform to connect customers with rides. As ride-hailing gets cheaper and more reliable, people will inevitably start to question whether owning a car is really worth it. The platform that already dominates demand is the one that is likely to benefit the most. In that environment, Uber stands to gain, with autonomous vehicles helping to lower costs and boost usage rather than undermining the model.

Finally, due to Uber’s established market share, it is possible that competition may not primarily be with other AV platforms, but instead occur between these providers on the Uber platform itself, where each individual provider will list their available fleets and compete for rider demand.

Quantitative Factors

The quantitative data increasingly supports the view that Uber's scale is reinforcing itself, translating network effects into durable growth and stable monetization.

Transitioning to the data, Uber's revenue growth has significantly outpaced that of the industry and is forecast to continue doing so. This is exactly what you would expect in a market where network effects matter, as scale increasingly translates into faster growth.

If this continues, Uber will gain an increasingly large market share, naturally improving its product, which in turn further increases market share. This creates a virtuous cycle, and that makes it progressively more difficult for smaller platforms to compete.

This revenue is mainly split between mobility (57%), delivery (33%), and freight (10%). Over the past year, delivery has been the fastest-growing segment, which is particularly interesting as this service is the least exposed to the AV shift. This is due to the additional benefit humans can provide that self-driving vehicles cannot replicate, such as in-store order pickups, delivery directly to the door, and ID verification.

Uber's take rate (Revenue / Gross bookings) is the share of customer spend the platform keeps, and this figure has remained relatively consistent since Uber turned profitable, sitting at ~30% for Mobility and ~19% for Delivery. This suggests that, despite high levels of growth, Uber has been able to sustain its monetization efficiency rather than sacrificing pricing or economics to drive volume.

Uber Technologies' free cash flow margin has been on a strong upward trend over the past five years, increasing from -40% in 2020 to 17% in 2025, with a TTM free cash flow of $8.7 billion. This shows a clear shift from a long period of sustained loss to one of meaningful, recurring free cash flow. The improvement represents the business reaching a level of scale where growth is no longer entirely offset by costs, allowing a greater proportion of operating profits to translate into cash.

Headline earnings warrant more caution. Reported net income is currently elevated by the use of accumulated tax losses from prior years, which artificially inflates earnings. As a result, the P/E ratio can make the stock appear cheaper than it is in reality. For this reason, valuation is better judged using pre-tax or cash-based measures rather than relying solely on near-term earnings multiples.

Risks

While Uber's operating performance has improved, there are several risks that could affect its margins and long-term growth trajectory.

Driver Classification:

One of the main risks currently faced by Uber is the classification of its drivers, with ongoing legal battles over whether drivers should be treated as independent contractors or reclassified as workers or employees. This exposes the company to continuously high legal costs and, more importantly, the risk of damage to its model if broader reclassification were enforced in other regions, as has already occurred in the UK. This change would leave Uber exposed to higher operating costs, including minimum wage requirements, holiday pay, and sick leave entitlement, and could potentially threaten the company's recently achieved profitability.

Even though driver reclassification is a real risk, the impact on Uber is often exaggerated. If drivers were reclassified in one region, similar rulings would likely extend to competitors operating in the same region over time, given how similar these models are. This means that Uber would not face any competitive disadvantage, but rather the industry as a whole would be affected. In this case, a portion of the impact would be offset by higher prices or take-rate adjustments in less price-sensitive markets.

Low Switching Costs:

A clear weakness in Uber's model is the lack of switching costs between platforms. Customers and drivers are able to switch between these platforms with ease, limiting platform “lock-in.” If a regional competitor is able to match Uber's service quality and reliability but offer lower prices, there is nothing stopping customers from using that platform. This risk can be minimized if Uber can increase the portion of users subscribed to Uber One, which introduces switching cost frictions through bundled pricing benefits and encourages users to remain loyal to Uber.

Autonomous vehicles:

As covered earlier, any shift toward AVs is likely to be gradual, capital-intensive, and heavily regulated, making it unlikely to disrupt Uber’s earnings in the near to midterm. Even in a more automated environment, it's likely that Uber’s platform would still play a central role in matching demand, setting prices, and managing customer relationships.

Regulatory Risk:

Uber operates in over 70 countries worldwide, each with its own laws and restrictions. This adds regulatory complexity and makes scale harder to achieve over the long term. Even if Uber comes out on top in the driver classification battle, jurisdictions can still make the model less attractive through tighter regulation.

Valuation

Uber is still in the transition from a high-growth, loss-making company to a more mature, cash-generating model, which makes estimating future margins difficult. Peer comparison also provides limited insight. Uber’s closest competitors are either smaller, at a different stage of development, or privately owned. As a result, I will focus on Uber's own historical scaling to estimate future margins, rather than relying on peer benchmarks.

There is a strong correlation between Uber's revenue and EBIT, which is expected given the mechanical relationship between the two. However, the graph shows that over recent years, EBIT growth has outpaced revenue, reflecting margin expansion.

Running a regression from 2021 Q1 to 2025 Q3, we get a revenue coefficient of 0.27. This means that within the sample period, a $1 increase in revenue is associated with ~$0.27 of additional EBIT, on average.

This sample size is relatively small (19), and the relationship reflects Uber's cost structure post-2021. Therefore, this model cannot be used for precise forecasting. However, it can show how incremental revenue has translated into EBIT historically and thus can provide a rough estimate of how EBIT will vary going forward, provided the current cost discipline broadly persists.

Applying this relationship to a 2030 revenue assumption of ~$87 billion implies an EBIT margin of approximately 17%. Plugging this margin, as well as the others implied using the same relationship, and applying a 9.5% discount rate and a 3% perpetual growth rate, the DCF implies an intrinsic value of ~$123 per share.

Current market pricing appears to focus on near-term cash flow volatility while underestimating the earnings power of a scaled marketplace model. Under conservative, normalized assumptions, I estimate a fair value range of $115–$125 per share, accounting for the variation in cost structure.

Conclusion

It is unreasonable to assume that Uber will simply be displaced by autonomous vehicles. Any shift toward autonomy is likely to be gradual and uneven, and it does not remove the need for demand aggregation, pricing, routing, or customer relationships. If anything, these functions become more important in an autonomous environment. Combined with long-term tailwinds such as continued urbanization, rising costs of car ownership, and increasing digital optimization, Uber’s platform is well positioned to benefit rather than be sidelined. From this perspective, the market continues to undervalue Uber’s long-term earnings potential.

I am interested to hear your opinions in the comments!

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Improves To 5-Month High
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment rose for a second straight month in January, reaching its highest level in five months. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index rose 3.5 points (6.6%) to 56.4 this month. Despite the improvement, consumer sentiment remains over 20% below where it was a year ago. The latest reading was higher than the forecast of 54.0.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regards to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month: a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment lifted about 3.5 index points this month, with minor gains seen across all index components. While the overall improvement was small, it was broad based, seen across the income distribution, educational attainment, older and younger consumers, and Republicans and Democrats alike. However, national sentiment remains more than 20% below a year ago, as consumers continue to report pressures on their purchasing power stemming from high prices and the prospect of weakening labor markets. Aside from tariff policy, consumers do not appear to be connecting foreign developments to their views of the economy. Note that interviews for this release concluded on January 19th, two days after Trump’s social media post announcing additional tariffs on eight countries in Europe.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. The current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 32.9% below its average reading of 84.1 (arithmetic mean) and 32.0% below its geometric mean of 82.9, based on data dating back to 1978.

To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In January, the CECI rose for the first time in six months to 55.4. This represents a 9.9% increase from the previous month but a -25.1% decline from a year ago.

Meanwhile, the CEI rose for a third straight month to 57.0. This represents a 4.4% increase from the previous month but a -17.7% drop from one year ago.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations fell back to 4.0% this month. This is the lowest reading since January 2025 but remains well above that month’s 3.3%. Long-run inflation expectations inched up from 3.2% last month to 3.3% this month. In comparison, readings ranged between 2.8% and 3.2% in 2024, and were below 2.8% throughout 2019 and 2020. As seen in the chart, uncertainty over short-run inflation expectations, as measured by the interquartile range of responses, has fallen from mid-2025 but has remained considerably elevated in recent months, comparable to levels seen in 2022; see today’s special report for additional context for long-run inflation expectations.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market cconditions,while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

data.title: QGRO: Robust Factor Mix But Mildly Disappointing Returns, A Hold
data.description:
In my opinion, the American Century U.S. Quality Growth ETF (QGRO), a passively managed exchange-traded fund I called "worth shortlisting" in September 2023, remains a Hold.

I have doubts that it will be capable of outmatching passively managed growth factor-oriented ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG), the one I believe is among the best in the league, and the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF), as well as the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ).

The gist here is that QGRO has outperformed the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) since the index change in May 2023, yet it has trailed SCHG, QQQ, and IWF, which track one of its benchmarks (as per the fact sheet), so this is quite a disappointment to say the least, especially assuming QGRO has a relatively high expense ratio of 29 bps.

At the same time, I appreciate the factor mix of its portfolio (namely the combination of value, growth, quality, and momentum). I think it might be supportive of delivering healthy gains in the current environment (i.e., on par or a little above those of the market, considering the growing economy, inflation trending down, the AI capex boosting sentiment, etc.). But this is insufficient for a Buy rating.

QGRO Strategy Review

As we know from the QGRO website, the core of its strategy is the American Century U.S. Quality Growth Index. Importantly, I should emphasize that before May 31, 2023, QGRO tracked a different index, namely the iSTOXX American Century USA Quality Growth Index. The indices might have similar names, but their methodologies have differences, so it's reasonable to ignore QGRO's performance delivered prior to the change.

As to the methodology of the American Century U.S. Quality Growth Index, I would say that it's rather intricate and demanding. As we know from the summary prospectus:

The Index universe is defined by the S-Network US Equity Large/Mid-Cap 1000 Index, which consists of 1000 large and medium publicly traded U.S. equity securities. The Index is designed to measure the performance of securities in the universe and identify those that exhibit higher growth, quality, and valuation fundamentals characteristics relative to their peers. To construct the Index, the index provider first screens the underlying universe and selects securities with higher profitability, return on assets, return on equity, and momentum. The index provider next calculates a growth score for each security, which is based on sales, earnings and cash flow growth and analysis of price to earnings and price to book ratios. The index provider then weights securities based on the calculated growth score.

Another fact not to miss is that the index and the ETF "are rebalanced monthly and reconstituted quarterly." As a consequence, despite being passively managed (which typically results in modest turnover ratios compared to actively managed counterparts), QGRO has a turnover of 172%.

QGRO Performance: Quite A Few Disappointments

What has QGRO been capable of delivering since the index change? There's something to appreciate about its results, though I'm still gravitating toward a view that they were for the most part mildly disappointing.

So we see that since May 31, 2023, benefiting from the bull market tailwinds, QGRO has quite easily outperformed the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF, but such growth-heavy vehicles like the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF, the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF, and the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF, which tracks its benchmark, as well as the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Fund ETF (QGRW), have all been doing notably better.

Next, over June 2023–December 2025, its annualized return was one of the weakest in the group (still higher than IVV's and just 3 bps below that of QQQ).

Besides, its risk-adjusted returns (measured using the Sharpe and Sortino ratios) were the softest, while its downside capture ratio (with IVV selected as a benchmark) was the highest.

QGRO, SCHG, and IWF: Portfolio Analysis

Does the QGRO portfolio have any notable advantages over those of SCHG and IWF at this point? In a word, there are a few things it does better, yet I would not say they are sufficient to construct a strong bullish thesis.

As of Jan. 21, there were 189 common stocks in its portfolio, key among them being Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) (GOOG), with a 4.08% weight. A nuance here is that it was represented by Class A shares only (that is, by GOOGL).

Though the QGRO portfolio has a notable overlap with those of IWF and SCHG, as shown in the two tables I provided, it is nonetheless unique in many ways.

Interestingly, while QGRO has exposure to seven trillion-dollar names, only two of them, namely GOOGL and NVDA, have made it to the top 10 group, while in SCHG, for instance, just two out of 10 key companies have sub-$1 trillion market caps (Visa Inc. (V) and Eli Lilly and Company (LLY)).

Sector Mix

Regarding sectors, while having a mix that at first blush is very similar to those of IWF and SCHG, QGRO still has something distinctive to offer.

More specifically, the key features are that it is lighter in IT compared to both while significantly overweight in health care.

Factor Mix

While I'm not particularly impressed by the returns QGRO has been capable of delivering since the index change, I should admit that it nonetheless has a few advantages on the factor front.

For instance, the QGRO portfolio has much better value characteristics than the selected peers, manifested in a 3.17% adjusted weighted average earnings yield, a 16.24% allocation to stocks with a Quant Valuation grade of B- or higher, etc.

Another advantage to highlight is that QGRO is sporting larger allocations to stocks with Quant Growth, EPS Revisions, and Momentum grades of B- or stronger.
