Final Thoughts

In sum, my opinion is that QGRO has an interesting strategy at the intersection of growth and quality. However, I would call the returns that it has delivered since the index change mildly disappointing, as it has failed to beat IWF, SCHG, QQQ, and QGRW. And even though its factor mix looks quite appealing, I would not be advocating for a rating upgrade, so it remains a Hold.

data.title: S&P 500 Earnings: Rotation In '26 And Major Tech Companies Start To Report Next Week
data.description:
Next week, starting January 27th, we start to hear from major tech companies: IBM (IBM, Jan 28th AMC), Microsoft (MSFT, Jan 28th AMC), Tesla (TSLA, Jan 28th AMC), and Apple (AAPL, Jan 29th AMC). Amazon (AMZN) was slated to release earnings Thursday, January 29th, but that now looks to be pushed back to February 5th.

For next week, my two favorites will be Boeing (BA, Jan 27th BMO) and IBM Wednesday night, which will likely be overshadowed by Microsoft and Tesla. (AMC is “after market close” and BMO is “before market opens” on the respective dates.)

Boeing’s all-time high was $446 from March 2019 and the stock today is still down 44% from that all-time-high. A separate earnings preview will be prepared for readers.

The article written last weekend, on the trend in tech earnings, was a lead into the tech sector’s earnings this coming week and how “momentum” investing – at least as it was learned in the late 1990s – sometimes acts as a vacuum and siphons capital from other sectors, often for long periods of time.

In the late 1990s, the market volatility was also much higher, where the S&P 500 and Nasdaq saw sharp corrections starting in 1997, with the Thai Baht and Malaysian Ringgit devaluations in July ’97, which led to a sharp correction in Q3 and early Q4 ’97, to the Long-Term Capital Management drawdown in 1998, which was frightening at the time since no one knew what was happening.

Because the late 1990s rally was very “binary” in nature, when the large-cap growth and tech began to correct, you’d see capital flow to all of the out-of-favor sectors that were doing nothing, like small and mid-cap, commodities, energy, utilities, etc., and these stocks and sectors would bounce for a few weeks/months, and then once the tech/large-cap growth rally would resume, those safer sectors would fade and return to dormancy.

In other words, investors saw a lot of “false tells” (rallies) from the laggards, ultimately to fade once the momentum trade reasserted itself.

This article from last weekend talks a little about the rotation being seen this year in the first 3 weeks, but we still have to wait and see what the technology/AI stocks report since good numbers and above-expectations guidance could reignite the Mag 7 Mag 10 crowd.

2023 and 2024’s 25% returns on the S&P 500 were all “AI” or momentum-driven. 2025’s +17.88% return on the S&P 500 was the first year we saw international participation with robust returns, as well as long out-of-favor gold and silver and – even late in the year – aluminum, copper, platinum, etc. In 2026, will investors see, “out with momentum, and in with value, etc.?”

One major difference between the late 1990s and the internet frenzy and today’s AI market is that market breadth has been remarkably solid through the 2020s, while in the late 1990s, the breadth data was not encouraging, even with five years in a row of +25% annual S&P 500 returns. My guess is any rotation will likely happen on a more subdued scale than 1999 and then the early 2000s, as could be happening now.

Of the 11 S&P 500 sectors in the S&P 500, per the LSEG data, technology is expected to see +31% EPS growth in 2026. Will readers be surprised to know that the sector with the 2nd highest expected EPS growth in 2026 is basic materials? Yes, basic materials is expecting 21% EPS growth in 2026. Basic materials is a whopping 2% of the S&P 500’s total market cap weight.

After updating the JPMorgan (JPM) and Citigroup (C) spreadsheets (still working on Bank of America (BAC)), both JPM and C reported decent metrics, and most importantly, the forward EPS and revenue estimates from ’26 through ’28 were revised higher, but the stocks trade very poorly. More needs to be written on this.

Finally, in last week’s tech earnings post linked above, I wondered how much of the “tech” sector was semiconductors and semi equipment. I threw an email out to LSEG and Tajinder Dhillon, the earnings guru at LSEG, told me that semiconductors and capital equipment combine for 38.2% of the tech sector. Intel (INTC) reported last night and had a very tough day today, so that will be a drag on the semiconductor sector.

38.2% is a big chunk, and the semiconductor (memory) stocks like Micron (MU) and Sandisk (SNDK) are white-hot right now. SNDK was up 112% YTD as of last night’s close, while Micron was up 39% YTD.

Like the warning on a pack of cigarettes, the semi sector, particularly commodity memory stocks, requires a warning to readers: these are very tough stocks to trade and to try to pick highs and lows. The bottom can come out of these stocks in a hurry, and semis can and have the ability to destroy capital for very long periods of time. This is a capital-intensive sector that is very cyclical. Between gold, silver and the memory companies, we’re back in late 1990s momentum mode.

Summary/conclusion

This blog’s biggest additions to client portfolios YTD are small-cap (primarily IWM) and Emerging Markets ex-China (EMXC) which has been a non-correlated long for a few years, but now the weight is being lifted. This blog is still adding to international for clients, even Japan via the EWJ. (More on this later.)

There are other opportunities too, like the energy sector and the aforementioned basic materials. It was interesting to read today, looking at LSEG’s “This Week in Earnings” and the Earnings Scorecard, that the energy sector is still seeing negative revisions, despite the sector finally showing some relative strength.

None of this is a recommendation or advice but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Readers should gauge their own comfort with portfolio volatility and act accordingly. Writing about the market helps me flush out the thought process and either cements certain views, or results in more time being needed to form a solid opinion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Evolution Metals & Technologies Is Just The Beginning; Caution Is Advised
data.description:
Evolution Metals & Technologies' Mission

This article discusses Evolution Metals & Technologies Corp. (EMAT), a critical materials and advanced manufacturing company headquartered in West Palm Beach, Florida, USA.

Evolution Metals & Technologies Corp., referred to hereafter as “EMAT”, was formed on January 5, 2026, through the merger of Welsbach Technology Metals Acquisition Corp. (WTMA) and Evolution Metals LLC (EM), according to Globe Newswire, dated January 5, 2026.

Also, the merged company, Evolution Metals & Technologies Corp. (EMAT), was scheduled to begin trading on the Nasdaq on January 6, 2026, under the ticker symbol “EMAT”. In fact, EMAT's stock closed on the Nasdaq on Wednesday, January 21, 2026, at $7.94, with a market capitalisation of $4.92 billion.

EMAT's ambitious project is strategically attractive to a sector of the US economy that has recently gained importance: the procurement of critical minerals and rare earth metals. This stockpiling is leading to geopolitical conflicts even between allies (for example, the US-EU talks over Greenland). EMAT's ambitious project is also commendable because it aligns with the trend toward sustainable economic growth with minimal environmental impact. Also, EMAT has a promising future, as its recycled products serve rapidly growing industries and markets with a long-term rather than short-term perspective.

Globe Newswire listed the company on January 5, 2026, stating:

The Company serves a broad range of market segments, including the automotive, aerospace, defense, healthcare, high-technology, consumer electronics & appliances, and renewable energy industries, while supporting a more resilient and sustainable business ecosystem.

About Evolution Metals & Technologies Corp.

Evolution Metals & Technologies Corp. is committed to strengthening the U.S. supply chain for critical minerals such as rare earth elements (REEs) and reducing reliance on foreign suppliers, particularly from China. EMAT focuses on providing these essential materials—such as REEs for magnets, carbonates, sulfates, and battery-grade pCAM—as well as precious metals through waste recycling. This process, known as “urban mining”, recovers valuable metals from discarded products such as electronics, batteries, and other waste products. Instead of relying on traditional mining, EMAT would like to use an environmentally friendly approach to extract these resources from the waste stream and supply the U.S. with materials such as REEs, gold, silver, and other essential minerals. China is unlikely to be pleased about this.

Promising Potential of EMAT Amid a Risk-Averse Market: Our View

There is no doubt that the supply of raw materials—such as rare earths, gold, silver, and other essential minerals—to the U.S. is a topic that resonates with the stock market, particularly given China’s likely discontent over this. EMAT, which was founded with an ambitious corporate vision, only recently emerged from a merger. Companies that positioned themselves early and proactively are now reaping the rewards of their efforts to gain market recognition. These companies secured venture capital and loans in time, possibly even before interest rates rose to curb inflation. In our experience writing about the U.S. stock market, we have encountered companies in similar sectors—critical raw materials and rare earths—that, while not strictly comparable to EMAT, are now recognised as strategically important to the economy. Compared to these companies, however, EMAT's future plans still appear immature. This includes taking a risk that seems less sensible in the current climate of global uncertainty and volatility than in growth phases where a higher risk appetite is encouraged by lower interest rates and economic expansion.

This project of EMAT, which requires substantial initial capital due to the capital-intensive nature of the industry, comes at a time when market sentiment is dampened by high volatility and uncertainty. Investors are struggling to predict the future economic cycle, and market demand for US equities is weak. We have certainly seen better times, such as when interest rates were low. Currently, market demand is uncertain, and with stocks like EMAT, investors may prefer to secure a financing commitment—for example, from banks—before making a final decision. There is concern that focusing solely on companies seeking to participate in the US supply chain for critical raw materials, particularly in sectors benefiting from geopolitical debates about electrification, environmental protection, or security, carries significant investment risks.

While EMAT could be an excellent long-term investment, it is still in its early stages of development, and substantial capital will be needed to progress further. Given the current risk-averse market sentiment—with the exception of stocks in the commodities or energy sectors—EMAT is considered a higher-risk investment.

This is especially true for investors who perceive the potential risk of investing in a company like EMAT as excessive, if not inappropriate.

Aside from commodities and energy, few sectors have outperformed the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY), which, according to GuruFocus, has risen 15% over the past twelve months. Alternatives are limited unless investors are looking for reliable, highly liquid dividend payers or defensive stocks. While these stocks are generally considered safe havens in high-risk markets and are adapted to the current economic situation, EMAT is much less suitable and requires a high risk tolerance that one cannot afford at the moment.

Given the current market volatility—where trends are rapidly changing, and global geopolitics are constantly evolving—the company carries significant risk. Market participants will therefore likely be advised to adopt a cautious, wait-and-see approach before investing in EMAT stock. The crucial question is whether capital will flow into this ambitious project and how much of it can actually be raised.

Financial Condition

EMAT assumes the following situation of the business, financial and balance sheet accounts on Seeking Alpha.

EMAT's income statement shows no revenue, only expenses, resulting in an operating loss. According to analysts at Seeking Alpha, operating cash outflow for the twelve months ending September 30, 2025, totalled nearly $8 million, while investments amounted to $10 million, likely including costs related to the completion of the merger. Cash inflows totalled nearly $33 million, primarily from the issuance of preferred stock.

The balance sheet shows cash and cash equivalents of $16.3 million as of September 30, 2025, which maybe indicate that EMAT can count on financial independence for a year or so. There is no debt, suggesting the absence of attractiveness for banks at the moment. Also, the company has a deficit of working capital of $112.3 million (current assets minus current liabilities), which contrasts sharply with the low value of its long-term assets of just $29.3 million. Objectively speaking, EMAT's total asset value is disproportionate to the business needs in this capital-intensive industry. The company must expand its fixed assets. Despite the increased risk and high cost of debt financing, this expansion will require substantial capital.

For the time being, the official website still lacks corporate presentations and information on the quarterly report release date. These documents could help clarify EMAT's financial situation. Aside from information about the technology and specifications of the complex recycling process—which aims to recover critical, high-value products for industries in electrification, the energy transition, and national security—the website offers little more than the reports filed with the SEC. The most important being the one filed on January 9, 2026 (Form 8-K/A: Current Report—Amendment), which includes the announcement of the completion of the merger, and SEC Form 4, by which Hansen Christopher James, a 10% shareholder in EMAT, resigned from his position as a director of the issuer on January 7, 2026.

Reasons for Risk-Tolerant Investors to Consider Investing in EMAT

Of course, it is risk-tolerant investors who pave the way for additional capital. They are, in a sense, the pioneers. Many are currently attracted by the potential for high returns on what is perceived as a risky investment. Once this growth phase is complete—during which some investors are also lured by speculation on a sharp rise in share price—the next phase begins, in which capital flows in from banks. In this phase, EMAT will make the leap into the next category. The company will then be considered trustworthy and secure the capital necessary for its economic development. EMAT will establish itself as an active player in the US economy with a clearly defined role within it.

Should this necessary preparatory phase occur, the positive momentum, which in our view will prompt most currently justifiably cautious market participants to reconsider their positions, could provide the much-needed boost, as they will fill these positions with optimism and enthusiasm. Given the illiquidity of EMAT stock and the average daily trading volume (3 months) on the Nasdaq of 51,291 shares (Seeking Alpha “ Trading Data”, as of today), the imbalance between the limited number of available shares and rapidly increasing demand will generate such upward pressure that the share price could rise significantly.

Risk-tolerant investors might consider investing in Evolution Metals & Technologies Corp. (EMAT). The company specializes in rare earth elements and other critical minerals in high demand for electric vehicles, renewable energy, and high-tech electronics. Given the US goal of reducing dependence on foreign suppliers—particularly China—and increasing self-sufficiency in critical raw materials, EMAT is likely to benefit from government incentive programs and support. While for the recovery of these materials through recycling is hard to gain traction under the current US President Trump administration's policies, it was presumably a focus for a Democratic administration, such as that of Joe Biden, the previous US president, who prioritized environmental sustainability. Continued support for green initiatives, including measures to improve environmental sustainability, is also expected under a Republican administration. But while these initiatives will not be entirely abandoned, the momentum is unlikely to be strong enough to impact stocks like EMAT, which has only existed as a newly merged company for a few days, and much development capital is needed.

A potential risk to EMAT's growth is geopolitical competition for mineral resources. The recovery of critical minerals through electronic waste recycling could reduce the need for traditional mining. This popularly known as “urban mining” could be seen as contradicting US foreign policy, particularly given US President Trump's ambition to control global mineral resources, as exemplified by his strategic posture with regard to Greenland. This poses a challenge for EMAT, as the company's focus on recycling rather than resource extraction could complicate its alignment with overarching geopolitical objectives. Furthermore, there is the risk that it could take a long time—possibly years—for US foreign policy to provide the level of support for domestic recycling efforts that is currently anticipated, and which would be sufficient for EMAT. Time is a critical factor for individual investors. If this policy shift comes too late, their capital will stagnate before it can have the much-needed impact and ultimately give companies like EMAT, which focus on environmentally friendly, domestically sourced critical minerals, a stronger market position. With this article, we would like to suggest, if possible, to those who have to invest as pioneers, that they focus less on the recycling of critical minerals from electronic waste and the complexity of the process and the sophisticated technology, and more on the strong growth of the markets that will be the customers of EMAT's products. It is undeniable that these markets are growing: The statistics in the screenshot of Canaccord Genuity Equity Research's “ Lithium Market Outlook” from November 19, 2025, show that global electric vehicle sales continue to rise sharply—up 23% year-over-year through October 2025—and that increasing installations are driving demand for energy storage systems. The Western world is also securing strategic minerals for the defense industry, as illustrated by the screenshot in the “ The Tanbreez Rare Earth Project” section of our article on Critical Metals Corp.

Evolution Metals & Technologies Corp. on the Nasdaq

Evolution Metals & Technologies Corp.'s Nasdaq-listed shares under the symbol “EMAT” were trading at $8.45/share, giving it a market capitalization of $4.71 billion, as of this writing.

Technical Analysis

According to Seeking Alpha, the “52-week range” is between $7.05 and $24.37. This range is not yet 52 weeks long, but is based on the lowest and highest share prices since earlier this month, when EMAT shares began trading on NASDAQ following the merger that formed the new company.

The current share price is 46.2% below the average of $15.71 (range: $7.05 to $24.37). The order of the moving averages (20-day, 50-day, and 200-day) is inverse: The 200-day average is above the 50-day average, which in turn is above the 20-day average. This suggests that EMAT stock is not only in a short-term downtrend but also shows signs of a longer-term downtrend. Many investors appear to have a selling attitude towards the stock or are waiting to see if high risk-tolerant investors will lead the way in generating broader, more positive market sentiment, and ignite the subsequent phase of banks that increasingly express confidence in EMAT shares.

The RSI reading of 49.69 reflects this atmosphere of indecisiveness and uncertainty—a wait-and-see attitude that typically represents a neutral pattern.

Valuation

During this time, we would wait until the company has raised the necessary capital. The offer price can then serve as an initial benchmark for valuing the shares. This implies a wait-and-see approach, in our view.

Risks

The risks are largely those we have already discussed in our article. Therefore, we will briefly summarize them here for those who have a position in EMAT or intend to acquire one.

  • EMAT is a young company, the result of a recent merger, which requires significant investment in a capital-intensive sector. This is necessary due to the company's current inadequate and precarious financial position, characterized by operating losses, negative working capital, and limited fixed assets. But also, the lack of financial reports and company presentations, which results in limited financial transparency for now, also increases the investment risk. Securing financing is proving tough as volatility and the high risk of insolvency, reflected in the Federal Reserve's high interest rates for federal funds, are causing investment restraint in the financial markets. The very weak US dollar is a further indication of the tense situation and exacerbates the problem. This dampens investor enthusiasm and makes financing more difficult.
  • EMAT's focus on recycling critical minerals (“urban mining”) may conflict with US geopolitical strategies that prioritize control of traditional mineral resources. Changes in foreign policy and government support could take years to materialize, delaying the company's growth and negatively impacting investor sentiment.
  • EMAT shares have low liquidity, which has negative consequences: difficult exit opportunities and the risk of poor performance, delays, or even remarkable losses. We consider the opposite improbable scenario, which is that: low trading volumes would generate upward pressure and drive prices higher. This would require a sudden, substantial influx of capital from risk-tolerant pioneer investors, among other things, implying risk of speculative early-stage investments. In our view, these investors are waiting to see how Trump's policies affect companies that want to implement projects like EMAT—that is, the recovery of critical minerals through recycling processes. This could also affect the timing of EMAT's market entry into the various markets and lead to a potentially serious delay. Until then, a general downward trend in the share price is to be persistent.

Conclusion

Overall, these risks suggest that most investors in Evolution Metals & Technologies Corp. (EMAT, Nasdaq) should adopt a cautious and wait-and-see approach. They should wait for concrete signs of funding or progress if they prefer a more strategic and patient approach.

Only investors with a high risk tolerance could possibly consider an early investment. For them, EMAT theoretically offers the opportunity to invest in a high-growth company in a critical sector—the recycling of rare earth elements and critical raw materials. Given the increasing demand for these materials, driven by geopolitical developments, clean energy, and technological innovations, including defense, EMAT could offer substantial returns for those willing to bear the risks of the early development stage, the high capital requirements, and market uncertainty. That said, it remains to be seen whether these risk-tolerant investors can create an alternative to underground mining – popularized by Trump’s slogans “Mine Baby Mine” or “Drill Baby Drill” – by focusing on future sales markets of EMAT rather than on producing these strategic minerals through sophisticated recycling processes. Politicians' rhetoric is not always used arbitrarily, but developing an opposing idea that contradicts what is currently considered a "slogan" by politicians can serve as a catalyst for generating new ideas that, while seemingly contradicting Trump's repeated statements, can in reality contribute to the development of new markets, since economic growth also flows through such things.
